missing_perspectives.what: Even RM100K-130K is not affordable relative to median income RM7,017. Charging access matters as much as price.
missing_perspectives.why: Analysis says millions lose but does not separate urban upper-middle from rural lower-income households.

missing_perspectives.what: Binding constraint is home charging, condo approval, wiring, shared electrical capacity.
missing_perspectives.why: Malaysia highly urbanised; many early adopters live in strata housing.

missing_perspectives.who: Chinese SME auto-parts vendors and Bumiputera SME suppliers
missing_perspectives.what: Fear sudden roadmap shifts, inventory risk, retooling costs, exclusion from EV supply chains.
missing_perspectives.why: Perodua alone purchased RM11B of parts from local vendors in 2025.

missing_perspectives.what: EV disruption is a skills and livelihood issue — fewer parts, different maintenance economics.

missing_perspectives.who: Women, especially commuters with care responsibilities
missing_perspectives.what: Safety, trip-chaining, school runs, dependence on public transport.
missing_perspectives.why: Gendered mobility research highlights safety and access barriers.

missing_perspectives.what: Peninsular industrial policy distributes benefits unevenly; East Malaysia faces different conditions.
Less about buying EV, more about future-oriented industrial base and green jobs.
missing_perspectives.why: Changes policy test from retail access to long-term industrial capability.

alternative_framings.evidence: Only 5,624 public chargers by end 2025 (56% of 10,000 target). AC chargers at 40% of target.

alternative_framings.alternative: Small-market scale problem with no signalled transition path
alternative_framings.evidence: UNCTAD: domestic market smaller than competitors, local firms less cost-competitive.

alternative_framings.alternative: State buying time with blunt instruments
alternative_framings.evidence: Critique is not that protection exists but that it lacks sunset clauses, milestones, retraining plans.
High-rise charging constraints mean gains accrue to landed/higher-income households.
Thailand/Indonesia/India all attach conditions but signal them predictably.

alternative_framings.alternative: Class issue masked by mass-consumer narrative
alternative_framings.evidence: Median household income RM7,017; rural RM4,588. RM130K is not mass-market.

international_parallels.lesson: Flexible incentives, not simple imitation. BOI allowed exports to count toward production requirements.
Phased conditionality — relaxed 40% local-content timeline to 2026.
Conditional but transparent: minimum investment, time-bound manufacturing, rising localisation thresholds.
Car-lite model, heavy public transport. Decarbonisation need not centre on private ownership.
Combined industrial policy, capability-building, technology absorption — protection only works linked to learning.

counter_arguments.counter: Overstates immediate demand. EVs only 5.2% of 2025 registrations. Chargers at 56% of target. High-rise barriers remain.
Foreign ownership stakes do not defeat the jobs argument. Relevant object is domestic production and supplier ecosystem.
Thailand/Indonesia also use conditions — issue is transparency and sequencing, not absence of conditions.
Private-EV-first approach reinforces car dependence. Electric buses, rail, transit-oriented development serve lower-income better.
Time-bound transition incomplete without measurable localisation, retraining, and innovation targets.

missing_facts.fact: 700,000 jobs includes 600+ component suppliers and 53,000+ aftermarket establishments
Only 5,624 public chargers by end 2025 — 56% of 10,000 target
Median household income RM7,017 nationally, RM4,588 rural
EVs were only 5.2% of 2025 vehicle registrations
CBU duty-free ended after 2025; CKD exemptions continue to end 2027
Perodua purchased RM11B of parts from local vendors in 2025
UNCTAD identified structural remedies: inter-agency coordination, outcome tracking, niche positioning
Civil society argued EV policy focused on private ownership reinforces car-centric model
92.3% of Malaysian households owned a car in 2024

gender_gaps.missing: Whether women benefit equally from cheaper private EVs given different commuting patterns and care work
gender_gaps.why_matters: Gendered mobility research highlights safety, access, trip-chaining issues.

gender_gaps.missing: Home-charging practicality for women in high-rise housing
gender_gaps.why_matters: Lower price does not solve strata-housing charging barriers.

gender_gaps.missing: Female workers in services/retail/admin linked to automotive ecosystem
gender_gaps.why_matters: Transition not only about male assembly jobs.

economic_gaps.why_matters: RM130K is not mass-market when median household income is RM7,017/month.

economic_gaps.missing: Separation of assembly jobs from supplier/aftermarket ecosystem
Charging infrastructure economics — AC deployment lag, DC cost RM210K per unit
Who captures first-wave gains from cheap EVs
economic_gaps.why_matters: Initial beneficiaries skew urban, landed, higher-income.

geographic_gaps.missing: East Malaysia as distinct infrastructure and affordability context
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Sabah poverty 17.7% changes meaning of affordable transition.
Lower incomes, different travel patterns, charging placement matters.
Clustering in urban corridors deepens regional inequality.

overall_assessment: Strong on elite policy framing and regional comparison but incomplete in four ways: treats consumers too generically, underweights implementation realities (charging, housing), does not historicise within NAP evolution, and lacks broader mobility lens (public transport, retraining, measurable targets).
